text.title: Cost-Model Spreadsheet Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules that turn a cost-model spreadsheet from a coverage gap into a defensible artifact.

rule.statement: The sheet MUST include lines for every category in the template, even if the line is zero: Compute (instances, autoscaling overhead, spot/savings-plan), Storage (block, object, backup-tier), Network (egress, NAT data-processing, VPC endpoints, public IPs), Operations (logs, metrics, traces, KMS, secrets manager), and Buffer. Marking a line "0" is allowed; omitting the row is not.
rule.rationale: The forgotten lines (NAT data-processing, egress, observability storage) are responsible for the typical 30-100% cost overrun. Forcing the row exists, even at zero, prevents the silent omission.

rule.statement: Every line item MUST populate four columns: unit (e.g., GB-month, request, vCPU-hour), monthly volume, unit cost, monthly cost. The volume cell MUST link to the Assumptions tab where the volume comes from (req/sec × 86400 × 30, or storage_GB × backup_count, etc.). Direct hardcoded numbers without source link are rejected.
rule.rationale: Hardcoded numbers in a cost model are unauditable. A reviewer cannot tell whether the number reflects an assumption or a guess; six months later neither can the original author.

rule.statement: Unit-cost values MUST come from the per-cloud-rates/ reference files (last_updated within 90 days) or directly from the provider's pricing calculator with the URL recorded. AI-generated unit costs without source are forbidden. Region MUST be specified per line (us-east-1, eu-central-1, etc.).

rationale.source: Cloud pricing volatility — list prices change 1-4x/year, plus regional pricing differs 20-40%

rule.rationale: A model with mixed-region or AI-recalled prices is wrong before it's signed. Region-aware sourcing prevents the largest class of unit-cost errors.

rule.statement: Scenarios tab MUST contain three: Conservative (50% of expected volume), Expected, Aggressive (200% of expected). Each computed from the same line table — not three hand-built columns. A flat single-number cost estimate is rejected.

rationale.source: Decision-analysis best practice; budget owners need a range

rule.rationale: Single-number estimates over-fit on one set of assumptions. The 0.5x-2x bracket exposes which lines are most sensitive (usually egress, observability) and informs the architecture itself.

rule.statement: The Buffer line is 10-15% of subtotal AND has a one-line policy note ("Buffer covers known-unknown unit-rate changes and small auxiliary services; not a hedge against missing line items"). Buffer > 25% is rejected — it indicates structural under-modelling, not contingency.

rationale.source: Project-management cost-contingency norms; cf. PMBOK Cost Management

rule.rationale: A large buffer is the architect's way of hiding gaps. Forcing it to be small and accounted for surfaces the missing structure.

rule.statement: Apply the spreadsheet template to one architecture proposal per pass. Skip this methodology entirely when cost is dominated by a single SaaS subscription — use the per-vendor calculator instead.
rule.rationale: Anti-conflation + sibling-methodology routing: bundling unrelated proposals into one sheet obscures per-architecture trade-offs.

example.title: Egress line correctly modelled
example.code: Line: AWS data egress to internet Unit: GB-month Volume: 5,000 (from Assumptions!egress_GB_month, derived 100 GB/day × 50 days/mo) Unit cost: $0.09 (us-east-1, AWS list, last_updated 2026-05-01) Monthly cost: $450 Notes: Excludes CloudFront-served traffic; that line is separate.
example.note: Sourced unit, derived volume, region named, cross-reference to a related line. Reviewer can verify in 30 seconds.

example.title: Hand-wavy line
example.note: No volume, no unit, no source, no region. Could be off by 10x. Violates r1, r2, r3.
